summary: Powertrain force flow provides the longitudinal context needed before adding aerodynamic drag and downforce.
Readers should already understand the basic solver flow before adding aero load and drag effects.
Drag, downforce, aero load split, and their lap-time tradeoffs are introduced using simulator behaviour.
Aerodynamics trades straight-line loss from drag against extra tyre capacity from downforce.

section: Drag and downforce in this simulator
summary: Drag and downforce both scale with speed squared, with drag dominating the baseline setup.

section: Aero center of pressure and axle load split
summary: Aero center of pressure sets front-rear downforce distribution and shifts tyre loads through wheelbase moments.

section: How aero couples with tyre and powertrain
summary: Drag reduces net drive force while downforce raises tyre load-dependent grip limits.
Higher downforce can still slow the lap when added drag outweighs cornering gains.
The simplified aero model omits ride-height maps and transient aerodynamic effects.
The powertrain model targets developers tracing how RPM and gearing become longitudinal wheel force.
First-principles powertrain force flow shows where wheel radius enters and where tyre limits intervene.
Lookup power data, drivetrain ratios, and wheel radius define the simulator's baseline traction calculation.
Wheel torque produces contact-patch force inversely proportional to wheel radius.
Engine power is read from lookup tables, preferring continuous power over peak power data.
Lookup power is converted to engine torque from rotational speed.
Vehicle speed maps to engine RPM through gear ratios, final drive, and wheel radius.
Engine torque is multiplied by gearing and transmission efficiency to obtain wheel torque.
Powertrain-limited longitudinal force equals wheel torque divided by wheel radius.
`wheel_radius` affects engine-speed mapping and torque-to-force conversion, with larger values reducing tractive force.
The solver tests each gear at a speed point and chooses the one with highest net force.

section: Coupling with Tyre Model and base_mu
summary: Net acceleration is limited by the lower of powertrain force and tyre grip, minus drag.
Monotonic tests enforce expected effects of gearing, efficiency, and wheel radius on tractive force.
The compact powertrain model omits shift transients, controls, inertia, and thermal derating.
New readers get a first mental model of how the lap time simulator assembles subsystem physics.
Quasi-static lap prediction combines tyre, powertrain, aero, and constraints into one speed profile.

section: What is a simulator trying to do
summary: The simulator predicts lap time and limiting channels from vehicle, track, and component assumptions.
Quasi-static modelling solves local equilibria algebraically instead of integrating full transient subsystem states.
Steady-state corner solves satisfy local force and yaw balances at each track point.

section: How we combine subsystems into one simulator
summary: The runtime chain builds models, solves cornering, propagates acceleration and braking, then integrates lap time.
Net longitudinal force comes from powertrain request, tyre limits, lateral grip usage, and drag losses.
Fast transparent trend studies trade away transient, thermal, and map-fidelity modelling detail.
The deep dive assumes readers already understand simulator basics and introductory tyre model concepts.
Tyre-force equations, implementation path, combined-slip handling, and modelling limits are examined in detail.
Contact-patch traction distributions integrate into resultant tyre forces and moments used by the lap solver.
Pacejka-style Magic Formula curves use fitted shape parameters to reproduce measured pure-slip tyre forces.
Vehicle construction and solver routines call the tyre model to supply pure and combined force limits.
`base_mu` scales load-interpolated peak forces while preserving the dataset's load sensitivity trends.
Combined slip uses a capped resultant force and rescales both channels when demand exceeds capacity.
A numeric example shows vector scaling of pure forces down to the combined-slip cap.
Effective loaded radius is the correct rolling length scale for slip and speed calculations.
Tests and analysis scripts check limiting behaviour, domain validity, load sensitivity, and track-level robustness.
The model omits transient tyre states, thermal evolution, aligning moment detail, and carcass dynamics.
Simulator Basics introduces the solver context needed before interpreting tyre slip, load, and force limits.
New readers get the minimum tyre-model concepts needed to follow force generation in the lap simulator.
Tyre force generation, `base_mu`, `wheel_radius`, and their role in the solver are introduced at a practical level.
The tyre model converts slip and normal load into the contact-patch forces the vehicle can actually use.
Slip angle links steering direction mismatch to lateral force buildup and saturation under fixed normal load.
Slip ratio links wheel speed mismatch to longitudinal drive or braking force before saturation.
Nonlinear tyre behaviour comes from force rising with slip to a peak, then saturating.

section: Inputs and outputs in this simulator
summary: The runtime tyre model maps slip angle, slip ratio, and load into limited lateral and longitudinal forces.

section: Why literature uses Pacejka style curves
summary: Pacejka-style curves fit measured tyre data so slip, load, and curve shape produce realistic force peaks.
`base_mu` scales the overall grip ceiling without changing the underlying tyre curve family.

section: Where wheel_radius fits and why loaded radius matters
summary: `wheel_radius` sets wheel-speed kinematics and torque-to-force conversion, so loaded radius is more realistic than static radius.

section: How tyre force is used in this simulator
summary: Tyre force caps powertrain demand, shares combined grip with cornering, and sets net longitudinal acceleration.

section: What this intro does not cover
summary: Equations, combined-slip details, and model limitations are deferred to the deep-dive lesson.
Aerodynamics introduces the final subsystem needed before combining the full three-pass lap solver.
Three passes impose cornering, acceleration, and braking constraints, with the minimum forming the final speed profile.
The three-pass envelope shows how corner, acceleration, and braking limits combine into one lap speed trace.
A near-straight segment can still be brake-limited because future deceleration constraints propagate backward.

section: Why the final profile doesn't look like any single pass
summary: The final profile switches pointwise between whichever pass gives the lowest permissible speed.
Backward braking enforces closed-loop lap entry conditions, so end-of-lap speeds need not match forward-pass limits.
Pass 1 finds the maximum speed satisfying lateral and yaw equilibrium under tyre force limits.
Pass 2 propagates speed forward using net longitudinal force from powertrain, tyre limits, and drag.
Pass 3 propagates braking feasibility backward so each segment can decelerate for what follows.
The final profile takes the lower of forward and backward constrained speeds before lap-time integration.
Runtime execution builds models, runs three passes, then combines speeds into lap time.
The capstone solver is quasi-static and steady-state, without suspension or tyre thermal transients.
Solver and tyre contract tests verify the three-pass workflow and returned diagnostics.
